lessons.title: 404 Not Found — Resource Missing or Wrong Endpoint
lessons.context: A 404 can mean either the resource genuinely doesn't exist (expected behavior to test) or the endpoint URL is wrong. Distinguishing between these two cases is critical — one is a test pass, the other is a test setup error.

problem.cause: Resource not found (expected) OR incorrect endpoint URL (test setup error)

antipattern.description: Treating all 404s as failures without checking intent
antipattern.why_bad: 404 is sometimes the correct response — e.g., GET /users/nonexistent should return 404. Failing on it blindly causes false negatives.

solution.description: Assert the status code that matches the test intent. For negative tests, assert 404 explicitly.
code: // GOOD — negative test: resource should not exist

lessons.ai_instruction: When generating tests for GET by ID, always generate both a positive case (existing ID → 200) and a negative case (non-existent ID → 404).

lessons.title: 400 Bad Request — Validation Error from Incorrect or Incomplete Data
lessons.context: APIs validate incoming data before processing. Missing required fields, wrong data types, or values outside allowed ranges all return 400. Tests must cover both the happy path and validation paths.

problem.cause: Missing required fields, wrong data type, or value outside allowed range

antipattern.description: Only testing the happy path without validation coverage
antipattern.why_bad: Validation bugs are common — skipping 400 tests means missing an entire class of defects
code: // BAD — only happy path, no validation test
const response = await request.post('/users', { data: validUser });

solution.description: Always test validation cases alongside the happy path. Check both status code and error message structure.
code: test('create user — missing email returns 400', async () => {

lessons.ai_instruction: For every POST/PUT endpoint, generate at least one validation test case with a missing required field. Assert both status 400 and the error response structure.

lessons.title: JSON Parse Error — Response is Not JSON
lessons.context: Not all API responses are JSON. Error pages, redirects, and some legacy endpoints return HTML or plain text. Calling response.json() on a non-JSON response throws a parse error that obscures the real problem.

problem.error: JSON Parse Error / SyntaxError: Unexpected token
problem.cause: response.json() called on a response that returns HTML, plain text, or empty body

antipattern.description: Always calling response.json() without checking content type
antipattern.why_bad: Throws SyntaxError that hides the actual response — you can't see what the server actually returned
code: const body = await response.json(); // Throws if response is HTML

solution.description: Check content-type header first, or use response.text() to inspect the raw response when debugging.
code: // GOOD — safe parsing with content-type check
throw new Error(`Expected JSON but got: ${contentType}`);

lessons.ai_instruction: When generating response assertions, check content-type before calling response.json(). For debugging unexpected failures, use response.text() to log the raw response.

lessons.title: Duplicate Data Error — Static Test Data Already Exists in System
lessons.context: Tests that create resources using static data fail on the second run because the resource already exists. This makes tests non-idempotent — they pass once and fail forever after unless the database is cleaned between runs.

problem.cause: Using static test data (hardcoded names, codes, emails) that persists in the database between test runs

antipattern.description: Hardcoding static identifiers in test data
antipattern.why_bad: First run passes, all subsequent runs fail with 409 — tests are not repeatable
code: // BAD — static data, fails on second run

solution.description: Always generate unique identifiers using timestamp or random suffix.

lessons.ai_instruction: When generating POST test data, always append Date.now() or a random suffix to unique fields like email, username, code, or name. Never use hardcoded static values for fields that must be unique.

lessons.title: Response Structure Mismatch — Data Wrapped in payload Object
lessons.context: Some APIs wrap their response data in an envelope structure like { payload: {...}, status: 'OK' } instead of returning data directly. Tests that access body.content directly fail because the actual data is at body.payload.content.

problem.cause: API response has wrapper structure {payload: {...}, status: 'OK'} — accessing data directly without unwrapping

antipattern.description: Accessing response data without accounting for wrapper envelope
antipattern.why_bad: body.content is undefined when data is at body.payload.content — causes cryptic errors

solution.description: Always inspect the actual response structure first. Access data through the correct path including any wrapper fields.

lessons.ai_instruction: Before generating response assertions, check if the API uses an envelope wrapper (payload, data, result fields). Always access the actual data through the correct nested path.
